channel.title: UGA CVM Faculty Publications
channel.description: New publications by University of Georgia College of Veterinary Medicine faculty, detected automatically via PubMed and ORCID. Includes department, authors, journal, and abstract for each article.

item.title: Are We Going to the Dogs? Human Disease Phenotypes in Canines — 2026

item.title: Assessment of Student Confidence, Knowledge and Attitudes after Use of Interactive Computer Modules in a University-Level Cardiovascular Physiology Course. — Advances in physiology education, 2026
item.description:
[Large Animal Medicine & Surgery] | PubMed Authors: Washburn Shannon, Ritter Nicola L, Davis Amanda R, Samson Joe, Moore James N, Ginn Zach, Brown Scott Journal: Advances in physiology education | Published: 2026-04-17 | PMID: 41996353 | DOI: 10.1152/advan.00209.2025
Many students struggle to master cardiovascular physiology due to the complex, dynamic nature of the subject and the cognitive demands of integrating theoretical concepts. Prior research suggests these difficulties are compounded by students' reliance on memorization, challenges in developing causal reasoning skills, and the inability to create acc...
https://doi.org/10.1152/advan.00209.2025
item.author: Washburn Shannon
item.dc:creator: Washburn Shannon, Ritter Nicola L, Davis Amanda R, Samson Joe, Moore James N, Ginn Zach, Brown Scott
item.dc:subject: Large Animal Medicine & Surgery

item.title: Assessment of hemagglutinin-inhibition activity following influenza vaccination during the 2022-2023, 2023-2024, and 2024-2025 seasons. — PloS one, 2026
item.description:
[Infectious Diseases] | PubMed Authors: Berber Engin, Hanley Hannah B, Gamez Brianna M, Ross Ted M Journal: PloS one | Published: 2026-01-01 | PMID: 41990070 | DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0347314
Seasonal influenza vaccines are available in multiple formulations including egg-based inactivated standard-dose, cell-based inactivated, recombinant, and live-attenuated. However, vaccine effectiveness and antibody responses vary by vaccine type, recipient age, and prior influenza exposures. Both age and repeated annual vaccination can modulate im...
https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0347314
item.author: Berber Engin
item.dc:creator: Berber Engin, Hanley Hannah B, Gamez Brianna M, Ross Ted M
item.dc:subject: Infectious Diseases

item.title: Rapid expansion of genotype D1.1 A(H5N1) influenza viruses in wild birds across North America during the 2024 migratory season. — Nature medicine, 2026
item.description:
[Other] | PubMed Authors: Harrington Walter N, Signore Anthony, Kercher Lisa, Giacinti Jolene A, Kandeil Ahmed, Ahlstrom Christina A, Bevins Sarah, Crossley Beate, Eure Karlie, Fabrizio Thomas P et al. Journal: Nature medicine | Published: 2026-04-15 | PMID: 41986738 | DOI: 10.1038/s41591-026-04300-1
In late 2021, high pathogenicity avian influenza A(H5N1) clade 2.3.4.4b viruses entered North America and reassorted rapidly with local avian influenza viruses. In September 2024, we detected a new reassortant later classified as genotype D1.1. Using active and passive avian influenza surveillance across Canada and the USA, we tracked the emergence...
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-026-04300-1
item.author: Harrington Walter N
item.dc:creator: Harrington Walter N, Signore Anthony, Kercher Lisa, Giacinti Jolene A, Kandeil Ahmed, Ahlstrom Christina A, Bevins Sarah, Crossley Beate, Eure Karlie, Fabrizio Thomas P et al.

Journal: Nature medicine | Published: 2026-04-15 | PMID: 41986738 | DOI: 10.1038/s41591-026-04300-1
In late 2021, high pathogenicity avian influenza A(H5N1) clade 2.3.4.4b viruses entered North America and reassorted rapidly with local avian influenza viruses. In September 2024, we detected a new reassortant later classified as genotype D1.1. Using active and passive avian influenza surveillance across Canada and the USA, we tracked the emergence...
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-026-04300-1

In late 2021, high pathogenicity avian influenza A(H5N1) clade 2.3.4.4b viruses entered North America and reassorted rapidly with local avian influenza viruses. In September 2024, we detected a new reassortant later classified as genotype D1.1. Using active and passive avian influenza surveillance across Canada and the USA, we tracked the emergence...

item.author: Harrington Walter N

item.title: Lysophospholipid signaling coordinates outer membrane homeostasis in Escherichia coli. — mBio, 2026
item.description:
Infectious Diseases PubMed

Authors: Enverso Gerald R, Trent M Stephen

Journal: mBio  |  Published: 2026-04-14  |  PMID: 41979331  |  DOI: 10.1128/mbio.00567-26

The Gram-negative outer membrane (OM) is an asymmetric bilayer that protects cells from environmental stress and antibiotics. This asymmetry, with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) in the outer leaflet and glycerophospholipids (GPLs) in the inner leaflet, requires coordinated synthesis of both lipid classes. The committed step of LPS biosynthesis is catalyzed by LpxC, a prime antibiotic target. Here, we sh…

View full article →
item.author: Enverso Gerald R
item.dc:creator: Enverso Gerald R, Trent M Stephen
item.dc:subject: Infectious Diseases

item.title: A single autoregressive integrated moving average with exogenous variable outperforms ensembles of autoregressive models for forecasting influenza hospitalizations in the contiguous United States. — Journal of the Royal Society, Interface, 2026
item.description:
Other PubMed

Authors: Arraes Rocha Felix Victor, Rohani Pejman, Drake John M

Journal: Journal of the Royal Society, Interface  |  Published: 2026-04-15  |  PMID: 41980730  |  DOI: 10.1098/rsif.2025.0813

Infectious disease forecasting is important to public health decision-making, particularly for mitigating the burden of seasonal influenza. We propose and evaluate comprehensive autoregressive modelling approaches (autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) and autoregressive integrated moving average with exogenous variables (ARIMAX)) for short-term forecasts of influenza-related hospitaliz…

View full article →
item.author: Arraes Rocha Felix Victor
item.dc:creator: Arraes Rocha Felix Victor, Rohani Pejman, Drake John M
item.dc:subject: Other
item.category: Journal of the Royal Society, Interface

item.title: Probiotic Prophylaxis for Enterococcus cecorum Prevalence in the Free Thoracic Vertebra of Broiler Chickens.
item.description: Authors: Jones Matthew K, Hofacre Charles L, Suyemoto M Mitsu, McKinley Enid T, Smith Alexandra H, Delago Jodi, Berghaus Roy D, Baxter Virginia A, Smith John A | Journal: Avian diseases | Published: 2026-04-01 | Department: Other | Source: PubMed
